coredata.dc:title: Green spectrofluorimetric determination of alendronate sodium using nano switchable probe; Water-soluble carbon dots synthesized from a natural source
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rizk, Mohamed
El-Alamin, Maha Abou
Mohamed, Dina
Toubar, Safaa
coredata.dc:description: Kidney beans were effectively pyrolyzed to produce N-carbon dots (N-CDs) with bright blue fluorescence. The mono-step pyrolytic synthesis was easy to carry out and resulted in water soluble green N-CDs with higher quantum yield 40.61%. Firstly, the fluorescence of N-CDs was turned off by Ferric ions (Fe3+); Alendronate sodium can recover the fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+. N-CDs worked as a highly specific detection probe to determine alendronate sodium in pure and pharmaceutical dosage form without interference from co-formulated cholecalciferol. The sensing system showed a good linear relationship to determine alendronate sodium within concentration (1.00−25.00μg/mL), with detection and quantification limits of 0.3μg/mL and 1.04μg/mL, respectively.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison between the properties of the proposed N-CDs and other reported N-CDs from natural sources

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: chemiical structure of alendronate sodium.
Preparation of N-CDs.
High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images of the prepared N-CDs.
FTIR spectra of N-CDs
UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 390 nm
Fluorescence spectra of (a) N-CDs (b) a +ALN, (c) a +Fe3+, (d) c +ALN, (e) Fe+3 alone, (f) ALN alone at λex 340 nm and λem 422nm.
The fluorescence signal stability over time after the addition of Fe3+.

(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ at λex 340 nm and λem 422nm, (b) SternVolmer plots (F0/F) for the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+‏ ions, the inset: linearity of Stern Volmer plot (Fo/F) against different concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.50−25.00 µM.

(a) Linear relationship between FL intensity and the concentrations of N-CDs at different concentrations of N-CDs, (b) Effect of concentration of N-CDs on the fluorescence determination of ALN, (where F is the FL of N-CDs-Fe3+−ALN and F0 is the FL of N-CDs-Fe3+) at λex 340 nm and λem 422nm.
Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ on the addition of ALN (1.00−25.00µg/mL) at λex 340 nm and λem=422nm.
Linear relationship between F/F0 and concentration of ALN (1-25µg/mL), where F is N-CDs-Fe3+-ALN and F0 is N-CDs-Fe3+ at λex 340 nm and λem=422nm.
Fluorescence of N-CDs with Fe3+ and some other metals at at λex 340 nm and λem 422nm.
Selectivity of the proposed method toward some other metals at λex 340 nm and λem 422nm.
Illustration of turn off-on strategy.
Comparison between the proposed method and the reported method for preparation of N-CDs from kidney beans.
comparison between the prepared and the reported CDs from a natural source.

row.entry: 450°C for 2 h the reflux with nitric acid for 10 h

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Parameters of the sensing system for the determination of Alendronate sodium.
Accuracy of the sensing system for determination of Alendronate sodium in pharmaceutical dosage form by the standard addition technique.
Precision of the proposed method for the determination of Alendronate sodium.
Application of the proposed method for the determination of Alendronate sodium in pharmaceutical dosage form.
Determination of Alendronate sodium in pharmaceutical dosage form by proposed method and the reported one.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Theoretical value for t-test and F-test.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Spectrofluorimetric method depends on the recovery of FL of iron(III) salicylate by ALN.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between the proposed method and the recent reported spectrofluorimetric method.
comparison between the recent analytical methods and the present study.

head.ce:title: Green spectrofluorimetric determination of alendronate sodium using nano switchable probe; Water-soluble carbon dots synthesized from a natural source

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, P.O. Box 11795, Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, P.O. Box 11795, Cairo, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Kidney beans were effectively pyrolyzed to produce N-carbon dots (N-CDs) with bright blue fluorescence. The mono-step pyrolytic synthesis was easy to carry out and resulted in water soluble green N-CDs with higher quantum yield 40.61%. Firstly, the fluorescence of N-CDs was turned off by Ferric ions (Fe3+); Alendronate sodium can recover the fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+. N-CDs worked as a highly specific detection probe to determine alendronate sodium in pure and pharmaceutical dosage form without interference from co-formulated cholecalciferol. The sensing system showed a good linear relationship to determine alendronate sodium within concentration (1.00−25.00μg/mL), with detection and quantification limits of 0.3μg/mL and 1.04μg/mL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphosphonates (BPs) are synthetic pyrophosphate analogues attached to the bone matrix with a high affinity [1]. These drugs have the potential to treat bone disorders such as osteoporosis [2], fibrous dysplasia [3], Paget disease [4], and primary metastatic bone cancers [5] by preventing bone resorption.

Alendronate sodium (ALN) is chemically named as (4-amino-1-hydroxybutylidene) biphosphonic acid monosodium salt trihydrate (Fig. 1 ). ALN is the 3rd generation bisphosphonate that promotes apoptosis in osteoclast cells by inhibiting farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase, a crucial enzyme affecting human tumor cells growth. ALN structure is devoid of chromophores, which would allow for sensitive direct UV or fluorescence detection. Literature review showed different analytical methods for the determination of ALN, such as indirect spectrophotometric methods with ferric chloride [6], orthophosphoric acid [7], ninhydrin [8], and chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole [9]. Indirect spectrofluorimetric methods with rhodamineB [10], orthopthaldehyde [11], and terbium [12]. Voltammetric methods [13,14]. Finally Chromatographic methods after derivatization with phenyl isothiocyanate [15], orthophosphoric acid [7], and Hantzsch pre-column derivatization [16].

Carbon dots were prepared in general by either bottom-up (microwave-assisted synthesis, ultrasonic synthesis, hydrothermal route, etc.) or top-down (electrochemical etching, arc discharge, laser ablation, etc.) approaches [17]. The top-down approach usually follows cleaving/ breaking down large carbon molecules to smaller structures, while the bottom-up approach includes building-up/step-wise chemical fusion of small carbon molecules by pyrolysis or carbonization. However, these approaches have several limitations, including tedious preparation methods, toxic starting material and time consuming process [18–20]. It is of great interest to explore the green sources of CDs because they are inexpensive, simple, clean, nontoxic, renewable, and conveniently available [21]. Many biomass resources, such as grass, strawberry juice, ginger, green tea, and coffee grinds, were employed for the hydrothermal preparation of N-CDs. Although these CDs can be synthesized from natural materials, most of them were created with a low Quantum yield or dispersed in an organic solvent. So, it was necessary from the standpoint of cost-efficient and green chemistry strategies to synthesize CDs from green compound that is quite available and cheap in everyday life [21].

N-CDs as a switchable probe were discussed by (Amine, et al.) based on (turn off-on) strategy. This strategy was achieved by studying the fluorescence intensity (FL) of N-CDs, then quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs with iron ions (turn off) and recovery of the fluorescence quantitatively upon the addition of the drug (turn on) [21].

The literature review showed only one previous preparation of CD from kidney beans with quantum yield (8%), prepared by heating at 450°C for 2 h, after pretreatment with nitric acid for 10 h and applied in cell imaging [22].

Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (Piochem company, Egypt), sodium chloride, and cobalt(II) chloride (El Nasr company, Egypt), chromium(III) chloride, barium chloride, acetic acid and potassium chloride (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany). Ferric nitrate (Loba Company, India). Red kidney beans from the local market. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments. Sodium acetate/acetic acid buffer solution (0.01 M with pH 4.5) was prepared by diluting the desired amount of sodium acetate and acetic acid in double-distilled water. 1.0mM ferric nitrate was prepared by dissolving 0.04g of ferric nitrate in double-distilled water.

Alendronate sodium pure sample (Sigma pharmaceutical company, Cairo, Egypt). Fosamax® tablet (Global Napi, Egypt, Cairo) contains 91.37 mg of sodium alendronate sodium trihydrate, equivalent to 70 mg alendronic acid, batch no. (U017500), Fosavance® tablet (Global Napi, Egypt, Cairo) contains 91.37mg alendronate sodium and 2800I.U cholecalciferol, batch no. (213035) from the local market

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) JEOL JEM -M2100 operating at 200 kV equipped with a Gatan Erlangshen ES500 digital camera was used to determine the particle size of N-CDs. The isolated N-CDs functional groups were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum, part no. L1600235, serial no: 49754). The UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs were recorded using JASCO (V-530) spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra throughout the experiments were recorded using JASCO FP-6200 Spectrofluorimeter with 10 nm slit width. Pyrolysis and stirring were done using hot plate WiseStir MSH-20D. Bench-top sonicator was used.

The kidney beans were rinsed with double distilled water to remove foreign materials and then left to dry at room temperature. The dried beans were ground to fine powder, 1.0 g was transferred to porcelain dish and heated for 25 min at 270°C on hot plate with magnetic stirrer. This burnt powder was left to cool, then dispersed in 100 mL double distilled water, stirred for 5 min at 1000 rpm, and filtered through a sterile filter syringe (0.22µm) after being sonicated for 10 min. A yellow solution was obtained (Fig. 2 ).

Stock standard solution of ALN (0.1 mg/mL) was made by transferring 10.0 mg of pure ALN to a volumetric flask (100 mL), dissolved well in double distilled water, and completed to the mark with double distilled water.

In series of 10 mL volumetric flask, 300 µL of N-CDs, 250 µL of Fe3+‏ solution (1.0 mM), and 0.5 mL of acetate buffer (pH 4.5) were mixed, and different aliquots from ALN stock solution were added covering concentration range from (1 to 25 µg/mL). The calibration curve was created by plotting (F/F0) against drug concentration (µg/mL), where F is the FL of N-CDs-Fe3+-ALN and F0 is the FL of N-CDs-Fe3+ at 422 nm after excitation at 340 nm.

For Fosamax®, Four tablets were finely ground, and an accurate weight equivalent to 10 mg of the drug was added to a 100 mL volumetric flask with double distilled water; sonicated for 30 min, filtered through 0.45 μm filter and proceeded as above.

For Fosavance®, Four tablets were finely ground, and an accurate weight equivalent to 10 mg of the drug was added to a 100 mL volumetric flask with double distilled water; sonicated for 30 min, filtered through 0.45 μm filter and proceeded as above.

The morphology and size of N-CDs was characterized with HRTEM. N-CDs are spherical in nature and not uniform in size as shown in Fig. 3 . The size distribution of N-CDs was performed via the statistical analysis of particles. N-CDs have an overall size distribution from 2 to 11 nm (Fig. 3(a,b), whereby most of the particles lie in the range of 6−8nm as shown in Fig. 3(c).
The diffraction pattern of N-CDs using HRTEM indicate that the sample is amorphous as shown in Fig. 3(d).

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to characterize N- CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , A broad stretching vibrational band at 3312.85 cm−1 was observed, which related to N-H and OH group, sharp peak at 2928.92 cm−1 related to C-H stretching, A peak at 1654.98 cm−1 and 1516 may be related to N-H bending, 1400−1236 cm−1 belongs to the C=C or C=N or C=O, and the peak at around 1000 belongs to C-H bending vibrational. The FTIR spectrum was matched with that reported CDs [21–23].
The UV-Vis spectrum of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 5 . An absorption peak at 270 nm was observed, related to Π-Π* energy transition, which belongs to the C=C bond .

Also N-CDs was characterized using Fluorescence spectrum. As shown in Fig. 6 , the excitation wavelength shifted from 270 to 390 nm, red shift of the photoluminescence peak was observed with decreasing fluorescence intensity, demonstrating an excitation-dependent emission of the N-CDs which is a characteristic property for CDs [21,24].

The quantum yield of N-CDs was estimated using Quinine sulfate as a reference standard in 0.1M H2SO4 by the following equation [19,25]:
Q =
Q
r e f
×
I
I
r e f
×
A
r e f
A
×
n
2
n
r e f
2
The quantum yield of the proposed method was found to be 40.61% which is better incomparable to the previous method (8%) [22].
Application of Analytical Eco-Scale and GAPI Tools for Greenness Assessment of the proposed method and the reported one for the preparation of N-CDs from kidney beans

Many greenness assessment tools have been developed. The analytical eco-scale was used to calculate a greenness score for each method by subtracting penalty points for each factor from total of 100 points. A total eco-scale score represents ˃ 75 (=excellent greenness), ˃ 50 (=acceptable greenness), and ˂ 50 (=inadequate greenness) [26]. Another method, the green analytical procedure index (GAPI), was used to assess the greenness of the technique by giving an accurate picture of the effect of each step of the proposed technique [27]. The GAPI tool uses a pictogram to evaluate the greenness of each step of an analytical procedure, using three different colors, from green to yellow to red. Comparison between the proposed method and the reported one for the preparation of N-CDs from kidney beans was examined. The results were shown in Table 1 . According to the analytical eco-scale metric, the proposed method is excellent greenness compared to the reported one (acceptable greenness). According to GAPI metric, the proposed method is greener than the reported method as in the proposed method only 2 regions filled in yellow as the process need filtration and waste result from beans powder not recycled. In comparison the reported method has 5 regions filled in yellow, and 4 regions filled in red as the method need filtration, usage of non-green solvent with large volume (100 mL), evaporation of the solvent, and usage of many instruments for several hours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the properties of the proposed N-CDs and other reported N-CDs from natural sources
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs prepared from different natural sources. Table 2 showed a comparison between different sources of N-CDs in terms of method of preparation, Excitation wavelength and Quantum yield. The proposed method showed higher quantum yield under mild conditions of preparation.

The prepared N-CDs have high fluorescence intensity, Fe3+ had a quenching impact on the FL of the N-CDs (Turn- off). This unique fluorescence quenching effect could be attributed to the Fe3+ ion's strong coordination with hydroxyl; and amine groups on the surface of N-CDs. Fe3+ has a higher affinity to N-CDs than others Metal ions; this may be attributed to orbital energy values of the lone pair of Fe3+ that lies between LUMO and HUMO of N-CDs. N-CDs act as oxidative photo-induced electron transfer [21]. It is found that ALN does not affect the fluoresce intensity of N-CDs. Fe3+ forms complex with ALN through binding to two phosphate groups of ALN. This synergistic interaction releases Fe3+ from the surface of carbon dots and causes an inversion of quenching effect (Turn-on strategy) (Scheme 1).

The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was examined toward alendronate sodium, and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. (7) , negligible fluorescence change was detected in the presence of ALN with N-CDs, so ALN did not effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Upon adding of Fe3+ to N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was quenched (turn-off). Alendronate sodium has the ability to recover the FL of N-CDs- Fe3+ through synergistic interaction with Fe3+ causing releasing Fe3+ from the surface of N-CDs (turn-on).

The literature review showed a high affinity between phosphate and Fe3+ in acidic pH due to the ionization of the acidic oxygen in the phosphate group [32]. In basic pH, competition between the phosphate group and hydroxide ions to Fe was observed. The effect of pH was studied using acetate buffer in acidic pH (4.20−5.60). Changing the pH does not affect the fluorescence intensity, so pH 4.5 will be used for further investigations.

The time-dependent FL quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ (Fig. 8 ), and the recovery of fluorescence of N-CDs – Fe3+ by ALN were studied and found that the responses to time were instantly and stable for an hour.

The concentration of Fe3+ was studied to avoid any excess amount of free Fe3+ that will react with ALN resulting in poor fluorescence recovery. So, determining the appropriate concentration of Fe3+ required to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs was necessary. Different concentrations of Fe3+ were examined, as shown in Fig. 9 a; increasing the concentration of Fe3+; decrease the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Addition of 25 µM of Fe3+ produced about 56% decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs which ensured the absence of excess free Fe3+ ions in the medium (Fig. 9 b).

The fluorescence emission of N-CDs was in linear relationship within the concentration range of 5.00 μg/mL−45.00 μg/mL; after that, negative deviation from this linear relationship was observed as shown in Fig. 10 a, which may be attributed to aggregation of many polar function groups at higher concentations of N-CDs [22]. The fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs-Fe3+ system in the presence of ALN was used to determine the optimal concentration of N-CDs within linear range. The results showed that the maximum ratio of F/F0 (where F is N-CDs-Fe3+-ALN and F0 is N-CDs-Fe3+) obtained at 30 µg/mL of N-CDs as shown in Fig. 10b. So 30 µg/mL of N-CDs will be utilized for all of the next measurements, and also used for the determination of the proper amount of Fe3+ required in section 3.6.3

Different aliquots of ALN solution were added separately to the solution of N-CDs-Fe3+ (0.30mg/mL N-CDs, and 250µM Fe3+) in 10mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with distilled water. As demonstrated in Figs. 11 , 12 . The fluorescence response (F/Fo) increased gradually with increasing ALN concentration. A linear relationship was obtained in the concentration range (1.00−25.00µg/mL of ALN).

Limits of detection (LOD) and the limits of quantitation (LOQ) were estimated according to the equation: LOQ = 10σ/S, LOD = 3.3σ/ S, (σ = the standard deviation of response and S = slope of the calibration curve). The low detection and quantification limit found to be 0.36 and 1.04μg/mL, respectively (Table 3 ).

The accuracy of the suggested method was determined using the standard addition technique, where an amount equivalent to 10 µg/mL of ALN in Fosamax® and Fosavance® was spiked with known concentration of pure ALN separately at level (50,100,150 The accuracy of the method was expressed as % recovery and it was found to be 99.45%±1.05, 99.21%±1.17 for Fosamax® and fosavance®, respectively as shown in Table 4 .

Table 5 shows the precision of the suggested method. Repeatability (intra-day precision) and intermediate precision (inter-day precision) were investigated by determining ALN at different concentrations in triplicate and on three days, respectively. The method proved to be precise with %RSD less than 1.

The specificity of the proposed method was investigated by determining its ability to determine ALN in its pharmaceutical dose form without interference from common excipients. The method was applied efficiently to determine ALN in pharmaceutical dosage form alone (Fosamax® tablets) or in co-formulated cholecalciferol (Fosavance® tablets) with % recovery±RSD (100.38± 0.88 and 100.05± 0.88) for Fosamax® and Fosavance®, respectively.. The results were shown in Table 6 .
Minor changes in the parameters that may affect the results were investigated using (pH 4.5±0.2) acetate buffer, Fe3+ concentration (25µM±0.5). No significant effect was observed.

The quenching performance was tested in the presence of 25µM different metal ions to discriminate selective fluorescence sensing of N-CDs towards metal ions (Ba2+, Co2+, Cu2+, K+, and Na+). According to Figs. 13 , 14
, only Fe3+ had a quenching impact on the FL of the N-CDs (Turn off strategy).

The developed sensing system was applied efficiently to determine ALN in two different pharmaceutical dosage forms (ALN alone and ALN with cholicalciferol) without any interference from excipients or combined drug (cholecalciferol). The results were shown in Table 4. The obtained results were also compared with the reported method using student's t-test and F-test. The results showed no significant difference between two methods (Table 7 ).

Determination of Alendronate sodium is a true analytical challenge because its structure lacks a chromophore and cannot be determined by direct spectrophotometric or spectrofluorimetric techniques. Some methods for ALN assay have been documented in literature reviews; the majority of them rely on derivatization stages with a time-consuming process and requirement of a large amount of solvents.

Although a new fluorescence spectroscopic approach for determining ALN was recently reported with nearly the same idea of turn on-off strategy [33], the proposed study has certain advantages in several areas. As shown in Table 8 , the proposed method is more sensitive than the reported method. The proposed and the reported spectrofluorimetric method were assessed using both the GAPI and analytical eco-scale metrics (Table 8). According to analytical eco-scale metric, the proposed method is greener than the reported method and due to the usage of green solvent. According to GAPI tool, the proposed and the reported method show 2 red zones due to the off-line sampling and transport to QC laboratories which occur due to the separation between pharmaceutical production and QC areas. Also the reported method shows 1 red zone in second pictogram and 2 yellow zones in third pictogram due to usage of non-green solvents compared to 1 yellow zone and 2 green zones, respectively in the proposed method. Also the proposed method was compared with the recent analytical methods. As shown in Table 9 , the proposed method is more sensitive than spectrophotometric, spectrofluorimetric and HPLC methods, except for spectrofluorimetric determination with fluorescamine which is relatively expensive with a narrow linear range. The proposed method is simple, selective, less time and solvent-consuming than the recent reported methods.

A one-step pyrolytic technique was used successfully to synthesize fluorescent N-CDs from kidney beans without any pretreatment. The characterization of N-CDs was investigated using UV-Vis, FT-IR, and HRTEM. Water soluble N-CDs with high quantum yield about 40.61% was used as a switchable probe in the determination of ALN. Fe3+ ions act as an efficient quencher to the fluorescence of N-CDs. The experimental conditions which may affect the results were optimized. By comparing the proposed method with the reported methods, it revealed that this method offered a valuable direct method for the determination of non-absorbing drug that lacks any chromophore in its structure and hence cannot be determined directly by spectroscopic or HPLC techniques. The proposed method could be considered as an alternative technique for the determination of ALN with various advantages as being selective, sensitive, simple, fast, and green with no need for expensive reagents or instruments. The proposed method was successfully used for selective determination of ALN within the concentration range (1.00-25.00µg/mL) and low detection limit of 0.36 µg/mL. This method could be used in routine analysis of ALN in pure and pharmaceutical dosage form without interference with the common excipients or co-formulated cholecalciferol. The present study was found to be greener in terms of usage of hazardous chemicals and solvents, energy consumption, and waste production.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have no conflicts of interest to disclose.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bisphosphonates induce apoptosis in human breast cancer cell lines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.G. Senaratne, G. Pirianov, J.L. Mansi, T.R. Arnett, K.W. Colston, Bisphosphonates induce apoptosis in human breast cancer cell lines, Br. J. Cancer. 82 (2000) 1459–1468. https://doi.org/10.1054/bjoc.1999.1131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis—where do we go from here?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Whitaker, J. Guo, T. Kehoe, G. Benson, Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis—where do we go from here?, N. Engl. J. Med.. 366 (2012) 2048–2051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pamidronate treatment of bone fibrous dysplasia in nine children with McCune-Albright syndrome

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Lala, P. Matarazzo, S. Bertelloni, F. Buzi, F. Rigon, C. De Sanctis, Pamidronate treatment of bone fibrous dysplasia in nine children with McCune-Albright syndrome, Acta Paediatr.. 89 (2000) 188–193.
R.G.G. Russell, Bisphosphonates: the first 40 years, Bone. 49 (2011) 2–19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic phosphate enhances sensitivity of human osteosarcoma U2OS cells to doxorubicin via a p53-dependent pathway

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Spina, L. Sorvillo, F. Di Maiolo, A. Esposito, R. D'Auria, D. Di Gesto, E. Chiosi, S. Naviglio, Inorganic phosphate enhances sensitivity of human osteosarcoma U2OS cells to doxorubicin via a p53-dependent pathway, J. Cell. Physiol.. 228 (2013) 198–206.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric determination of alendronate in pharmaceutical formulations via complex formation with Fe (III) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kuljanin, I. Janković, J. Nedeljković, D. Prstojević, V. Marinković, Spectrophotometric determination of alendronate in pharmaceutical formulations via complex formation with Fe (III) ions, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal.. 28 (2002) 1215–1220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic and HPLC methods for the determination of alendronate in tablets and urine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Al Deeb, I.I. Hamdan, S.M. Al Najjar, Spectroscopic and HPLC methods for the determination of alendronate in tablets and urine, Talanta. 64 (2004) 695–702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric determination of alendronate sodium in bulk drug and in pharmaceutical formulation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.A. Alarfaj, S.A. Abdel-Razeq, F.N. Alqahtani, Spectrophotometric determination of alendronate sodium in bulk drug and in pharmaceutical formulation, Asian J. Chem.. 23 (2011) 697–700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validated spectrophotometric methods for determination of Alendronate sodium in tablets through nucleophilic aromatic substitution reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.I. Walash, M.E.S. Metwally, M. Eid, R.N. El-Shaheny, Validated spectrophotometric methods for determination of Alendronate sodium in tablets through nucleophilic aromatic substitution reactions, Chem. Cent. J.. 6 (2012) 1–15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrofluorimetric determination of alendronate by conjugation with the rhodamine B sulfonyl group

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-S. Jeong, J.-H. Park, G. Jin, J.-S. Park, Spectrofluorimetric determination of alendronate by conjugation with the rhodamine B sulfonyl group, Bull. Korean Chem. Soc.. 32 (2011) 1777–1780.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fast and simple spectrofluorometric method for the determination of alendronate sodium in pharmaceuticals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.E.N. Dolatabadi, H. Hamishehkar, M. de la Guardia, H. Valizadeh, A fast and simple spectrofluorometric method for the determination of alendronate sodium in pharmaceuticals, BioImpacts: BI. 4 (2014) 39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of terbium-sensitized fluorescence method for the determination of alendronate in biological samples followed by magnetic solid-phase extraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Niaei, A. Samadi, H. Hamishehkar, M. Ghorbani, Development of terbium-sensitized fluorescence method for the determination of alendronate in biological samples followed by magnetic solid-phase extraction, Microchem. J.. 146 (2019) 888–894.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric investigation and amperometric detection of the bisphosphonate drug sodium alendronate using a copper nanoparticles-modified electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Heli, F. Faramarzi, N. Sattarahmady, Voltammetric investigation and amperometric detection of the bisphosphonate drug sodium alendronate using a copper nanoparticles-modified electrode, J. Solid State Electrochem.. 14 (2010) 2275–2283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electrochemical determination for the anti-osteoporosis drug (alendronate sodium) and its application for corrosion monitoring of surgical 316 L SS bone implant
